question.text: Apex Corporation has post-issue paid-up capital of Rs.50 crore. The promoters hold Rs.12 crore worth of shares post-issue. To meet the minimum promoter contribution requirement, how much shortfall exists and what is the maximum any single AIF can contribute?

answer.text: Shortfall of Rs.2 crore; maximum AIF contribution of Rs.5 crore
No shortfall; promoters already exceed the 20% requirement
Shortfall of Rs.2 crore; maximum AIF contribution of Rs.1 crore
Shortfall of Rs.3 crore; maximum AIF contribution of Rs.5 crore

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Minimum promoter contribution = 20% of Rs.50 crore = Rs.10 crore. Promoters hold Rs.12 crore, which exceeds the Rs.10 crore requirement. There is NO shortfall. The question about AIF contribution is therefore moot -- no shortfall means no need for shortfall contributors. Each shortfall contributor can contribute maximum 10% of post-issue capital (Rs.5 crore here), but this is irrelevant since there is no shortfall. **The Trap:** This is a trick question. Candidates may rush to calculate a shortfall without first checking whether one exists. Rs.12 crore is MORE than Rs.10 crore (20% of Rs.50 crore). Always verify the basic condition before jumping to shortfall calculations. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series II-A Chapter 7 covers promoter contribution and shortfall mechanisms. RTAs must verify these calculations during pre-issue processing. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Promoters holding more than the minimum (24% vs 20% here) is generally a positive signal -- it shows stronger promoter commitment. Track the exact promoter contribution percentage as a quality indicator.
